EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent cluster of heavy showers and thunderstorms moved down the coast of St. Lucie and Martin Counties and produced a localized area of very high winds within the town of Hobe Sound. Twenty mobile homes sustained mainly minor wind damage, with moderate damage to two homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey confirmed a zone of straight-line wind damage approximately aligned northeast to southwest, approximately one-third of a mile long and up to about 300 feet wide, affecting the Woodbridge Mobile Home park in the town of Hobe Sound. Twenty mobile homes sustained wind damage. Carports and awnings were removed from homes, and several homes had parts of their metal roofs peeled back. Two large trees sustained damage, primarily from debris impact. Damage estimates totalled $150,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low level trough extended across the Okefenokee Swamp southward across the Suwanee River Valley, which triggered early morning slow moving showers and thunderstorms across Columbia county. A moisture feed off of the Gulf of Mexico due to low level SSW winds and spokes of energy rotating around a low center meandering over the local area fueled slow moving convection, which caused flash flooding over Columbia county generally between the cities of Columbia and Lake City. Daily storm total amounts ranged from 7 to almost 10.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey revealed flash flooding caused a home off of SW Edward Terrace was flooded out (about 2 inches of water in the home) with sewage in the backyard. This occurred around 7 am.  Around 6 am, a retention pone was flooded out and surrounded several homes off of SW Nightshade Drive about 3 miles NE of the city of Columbia. Around the same time, another retention pond off of Fall Court surpassed bankfull and flooded a garage. Several inches of flood water covered roadways throughout Columbia county. The cost of damage was unknown, but a value was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall line moved eastward across the area during the afternoon hours of March 28th.  Strong onshore flow ahead of the line combined with a significant pressure fall and rise in association with the passage of the line produced minor coastal flooding around Panama City.  The Panama City tide gauge rose approximately 3 feet very rapidly to a level of 3.75 ft, which is above the minor flood stage of 3.4 feet.  It then quickly fell below flood stage.  Minor damage to beach equipment and outbuildings was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail reported at the Saint Lucie County International Airport (KFPR).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed out ahead of a cold front over the eastern Gulf of Mexico. Thunderstorms within the squall line produced damaging winds and large hail, mainly within Lake, Seminole and Osceola Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A WeatherFlow Station in New Smyrna Beach reported a wind gust of 60 mph out of the WNW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed out ahead of a cold front over the eastern Gulf of Mexico. Thunderstorms within the squall line produced damaging winds and large hail, mainly within Lake, Seminole and Osceola Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line westerly winds estimated between 60 and 75 mph produced damage in St. Cloud, along the leading edge of a rapidly advancing squall line. Approximately a dozen homes experienced mainly minor damage, to soffits and roof shingles. Several trees, fences and powerlines were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms over southeast Brevard County produced wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph as they moved quickly northeast into the Atlantic. Minor wind damage occurred to a mobile home in Palm Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Palm Bay citizen reported that strong thunderstorm winds damaged the aluminum porch of his neighbors mobile home on Tangelo Drive and peeled back part of the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep mid-level lapse rates and an upper level trough moving through combined to produce a few severe storms across the area with isolated large hail and damaging winds. In addition, training cells across Panama City produced some reports of flash flooding with over 3 inches of rain measured in just a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail slightly smaller than a golf ball was reported near Apalachicola.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was submitted on social media of damage to an awning attached to a house near Panama City Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall line developed ahead of a low pressure center and associated cold front over the Gulf of Mexico that moved onshore during the early afternoon of the 6th. The thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and two waterspouts that moved onshore as tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports were received from the public of several large branches down, as well as fence and shed damage between Largo and Belleair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass moved southward into the area, causing temperatures to plunge on the mornings of Jan 7-8.  A hard freeze occurred over the entire forecast area on both mornings, and wind chills were in the single digits and teens on the morning on Jan 7th.  As a result of the cold temperatures, several pipes burst on the FSU campus, resulting in damages of greater than $1 million as reported by the FSU Emergency Manager.

A squall line moved across northwest Florida during the morning hours of March 16th.  Although no reports of severe weather were received, lightning did damage a home.  In addition, heavy rainfall of 3-5 inches caused some rivers and streams to come out of their banks with areal flooding reported in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning destroyed a home on Oak Forest Road in the Hiland Park area of Lynn Haven.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across northwest Florida during the morning hours of March 16th.  Although no reports of severe weather were received, lightning did damage a home.  In addition, heavy rainfall of 3-5 inches caused some rivers and streams to come out of their banks with areal flooding reported in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches during March 16th saturated the ground and produced flooding around the town of Mayo.  A local state of emergency was declared by the town due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure and its associated strong cold front moved across South Florida causing an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop over northeast Miami-Dade County and move into southern Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Opa-Locka Executive Airport reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 knots, or 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure and its associated strong cold front moved across South Florida causing an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop over northeast Miami-Dade County and move into southern Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report relayed through Social Media reported dime sized hail in Miami Gardens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter storm to impact the NWS Tallahassee County warning area in five years brought a wintry mix of precipitation to virtually the entire forecast area. The predominant precipitation types were sleet and freezing rain.  Total liquid equivalent estimates were estimated to be greater than a quarter inch across portions of most of the Florida Panhandle counties.  Several roads were closed, including a large stretch of Interstate 10 in the Florida Panhandle. Most bridges were closed at one point from Tallahassee westward, and during the peak of the event, there was no road access to cross over the Apalachicola River. This led to very large transportation impacts with significant monetary losses for some trucking companies.  Even the bridge going to St. George Island was closed at one point due to ice!||On the morning of January 28th, there was a trough approaching from the west with moist southwest flow and precipitation ahead of it over the Southeast. At the surface, a cold front was stalled across north central Florida and the Gulf of Mexico. However, the deep arctic air was still well north of our region. During the day, the precipitation changed over to snow and/or sleet in places like Atlanta, Birmingham, Macon, and Mobile, as progressively colder air flowed into the region. This caused major traffic problems, especially in Atlanta. ||Late in the afternoon and early evening the rain began to change to freezing rain and sleet in portions of Southeast Alabama, Southwest Georgia, and western portions of the Florida Panhandle. There appeared to be a secondary cold front (a fairly common occurrence when arctic air moves southward) that helped accelerate the advance of the cold air and changeover to freezing/frozen precipitation during the evening. ||During the night (January 28th ��� January 29th) the cold air continued to spread south and east. By the morning of January 29th, many locations in our forecast area had changed to either freezing rain, sleet, or a mix, including Tallahassee. Much drier air in the mid to upper atmosphere overspread Southeast Alabama and Southwest Georgia. This caused the precipitation to end there during the morning hours. However, light wintry precipitation lingered across the Florida Big Bend throughout the day, as the upper level trough continued to approach from the west. The combination of the unusually cold air and thick clouds prevented many locations from getting above freezing, a very rare occurrence in the Deep South. ||The Wednesday morning surface chart showed the main cold front finally on the move again through South Florida, while high pressure and very cold, dry air continued to infiltrate the Gulf Coast Region. This precipitation tapered off during the evening as the atmosphere dried out.

An upper level trough of low pressure and its associated strong cold front moved across South Florida causing an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop over northeast Miami-Dade County and move into southern Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report relayed through Social Media reported hail at least the size of quarters in Carol City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 12th generated a squall line that produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts and small hail.  Additionally, a lightning strike from the squall line started a fire at an apartment complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of a section of a wood fence blown out near the intersection of Decubellis Road and Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 12th generated a squall line that produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts and small hail.  Additionally, a lightning strike from the squall line started a fire at an apartment complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Department of Emergency Management relayed a report of a fire started by a lightning strike at an apartment complex. The fire caused smoke damage to 8 units of the apartment building, located near White Swan Drive and Twin Lakes Boulevard. A damage estimate of $10,000 was determined by taking a low end range of average smoke damage claims in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sudden wind increase associated with a passing cold front swept through east-central Florida early on March 13. The winds steepened waves, swamped a fishing boat, and resulting in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sudden wind increase following passage of a cold front led to steepening waves over open waters. A fishing boat within the Stick Marsh in Indian River County was swamped by the waves, estimated at two to three feet. There were three occupants onboard the boat. One person succumbed to the cold water and likely died from hypothermia. The other two persons also had hypothermia, but refused hospital treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 12th generated a squall line that produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts and small hail.  Additionally, a lightning strike from the squall line started a fire at an apartment complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey team found straight line wind damage in Fort Meade, while investigating an unverified tornado report.  The survey team found a metal roof to a patio blown over the home and into the back yard.  All of the debris was blown in a uniform direction, leading the team to conclude the damage was from straight line winds and not a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 12th generated a squall line that produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts and small hail.  Additionally, a lightning strike from the squall line started a fire at an apartment complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk County emergency management and broadcast media reported that 17 homes sustained roof damage at the Davenport RV Resort along Powerline Road in Haines City. Three of these mobile homes had structural damage, though the others had only superficial damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 12th generated a squall line that produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts and small hail. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk County emergency management relayed a report of 2 houses sustaining roof damage at Tate Road and West Easy Street in Bartow.  Time was estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hernando County Sheriff's Office reported a large tree fell on a house near Brooksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere along a stalled frontal boundary fueled strong thunderstorms over the Nature Coast. One of these storms produced hail the size of quarters in Levy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail near Williston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate east to southeast winds led to rip current formation along the southeast Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A young man (Jacobo Dorcely) drowned while swimming at Hammock Park near Boynton Inlet. The man's age was not indicated in media reports, but is estimated to be around 18 years of age. His body was recovered about 50 feet from shore. Occurred at an unguarded beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Building high pressure over the Atlantic resulted in onshore flow along the Florida east coast and rip currents at  the beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that a 27-year old male was pulled out to sea from a rip current. The Coast Guard has suspended the search and the man is presumed dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance combined with elevated instability produced a few severe storms along the northern periphery of a warm front located from Flagler county westward to Marion and Alachua county in the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail damage vehicles near Lake Disston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong high pressure over Canada and a coastal trough offshore brought a very strong pressure gradient along the local coastline. Wind gusts of 30-40 mph and persistent onshore flow combined with high tides brought waves of coastal flooidng and beach erosion, especially to the South Ponte Vedra beach coastline which is historically problematic during strong NE flow events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surf heights reported up to 6-8 ft with pockets of minor beach erosion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area on January 11th.  Limited instability precluded a more widespread outbreak of severe storms, but low level winds and shear were very strong, and some storms did produce straight line wind damage with mainly trees and power lines being blown down.  Gadsden and Wakulla counties were hit the hardest with numerous instances of trees and lines being blown down.  Talquin Electric had over 7000 customers without power at its peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down on Cajer Posey Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough brought numerous showers and thunderstorms to the tri-state area just before Christmas.  Thunderstorms trained over the city of Tallahassee, resulting in major flash flooding with water rescues and numerous stranded vehicles.  An unseasonably moist environment was in place with TAE reporting its third highest precipitable water value on record for December during this event.  The Tallahassee Airport measured 7.44 of rain on the 23rd, which made it Tallahassee's wettest December day on record, and 9th wettest calendar day on record overall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The underpass at Pat Thomas Blvd at West Tennessee Street was flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported along King and Malaga Streets in St Augustine. Minor street flooding was reported around Maria Sanchez Lake and Avenieda Menedez Road had standing salt water in parking spaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong high pressure over Canada and a coastal trough offshore brought a very strong pressure gradient along the local coastline. Wind gusts of 30-40 mph and persistent onshore flow combined with high tides brought waves of coastal flooidng and beach erosion, especially to the South Ponte Vedra beach coastline which is historically problematic during strong NE flow events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water pooled around the Porpoise Point are in South Ponte Vedra during the high tide. The beach and vehicular access ramps flooded during the high tide. Beach erosion estimates were up to 35 ft in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe storms formed along a lifting warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought record flooding along with severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and tornadoes to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central and southern Escambia county experienced historic flooding the night of April 29th into the early morning of April 30th. Nearly 2 feet of rain fell across portions of   southern Escambia County. Pensacola International Airport recorded a storm total of 20.47 inches. 1687 residential homes suffered major flooding with 12 inches of water or more. 1518 residential homes suffered minor flooding with less than 12 inches of water. 13 homes were destroyed. 117 commercial businesses experienced major damage. ||Widespread infrastructure damage, totaling $90 million, occurred with numerous roads, bridges, and drainage systems heavily damaged or destroyed. ||Numerous water rescues took place during the height of the event due to the rapid rise of water. A 67 year old Pensacola woman drowned when her vehicle was submerged by flood waters on U.S. 29 near Cantonment, FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and isolated thunderstorms moved very slowly down the coast of St. Lucie and Martin Counties during the afternoon and early evening. Twenty-four hour rainfall totals reached 8-12 inches across much of the coastal portion of the counties, with the majority of the rain falling in a period of 6-hours or less. Flooding of many roadways occurred, stranding vehicles. Drainage canals overflowed and standing water reached a foot in many locations and was slow to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar-based rainfall estimates were between 6 and 12 inches across eastern-most Martin County, with most of the rain falling in a 6-hour or less period. The twenty-four hour rain gage total at Stuart was 10.55 inches and 12.00 inches at Nettles Island, but with most of the rain falling in less than 6 hours. Flooding closed many roadways, stranding vehicles. Drainage canals and creeks overflowed. While high water surrounded many subdivisions, businesses and homes, water only entered one building, a Martin County High School, causing an estimated $220,000 in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.  Isolated supercells developed ahead of the main squall line, and one produced an EF2 tornado in Florida which was the longest continuous damage path in Florida since the Central Florida outbreak of February 1, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornadic debris signature was seen on dual-pol radar at this location.  This is a rural and heavily forested area with no structural damage reported and poor road access.

Thunderstorms trained over the city of Tallahassee, resulting in major flash flooding with water rescues and numerous stranded vehicles.  An unseasonably moist environment was in place with TAE reporting its third highest precipitable water value on record for December during this event.  The Tallahassee Airport measured 7.44 of rain on the 23rd, which made it Tallahassee's wettest December day on record, and 9th wettest calendar day on record overall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles were stuck in water and blocking traffic at the intersection of S Blairstone Road and St Augustine Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and isolated thunderstorms moved very slowly down the coast of St. Lucie and Martin Counties during the afternoon and early evening. Twenty-four hour rainfall totals reached 8-12 inches across much of the coastal portion of the counties, with the majority of the rain falling in a period of 6-hours or less. Flooding of many roadways occurred, stranding vehicles. Drainage canals overflowed and standing water reached a foot in many locations and was slow to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar-based rainfall estimates were between 6 and 12 inches across eastern-most St. Lucie County, with most of the rain falling in a 6-hour or less period. The twenty-four hour rain gage total at Ft. Pierce was 10.64 inches, but with most of the rain falling in less than 6 hours. Flooding closed many roadways, stranding vehicles. Drainage canals and creeks overflowed. While high water surrounded many subdivisions, businesses and homes, water was only reported to have entered one apartment complex in Ft. Pierce (three separate buildings). Damage estimated totalled over 20,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.  Isolated supercells developed ahead of the main squall line, and one produced an EF2 tornado in Florida which was the longest continuous damage path in Florida since the Central Florida outbreak of February 1, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado likely began in a heavily forested area with limited access just SW of the Calhoun Correctional Institution - between the prison grounds and Highway 71. The beginning point was estimated based on the rest of the damage path and a lack of observed damage along Highway 71. The tornado then quickly intensified as it moved into the prison grounds. Several concrete buildings were heavily damaged - including a two-story printing facility. The eastern wall of that particular building was completely collapsed with the roof almost entirely removed. An adjacent concrete building also had large sections of roof removed. The damage to both of these buildings was rated EF2 with maximum estimated winds of 120-130 mph - in the high end of the EF2 range. Other buildings, including some warehouses, were also damaged on the prison grounds. There were some light poles that were snapped, and some trees near the entrance to the facility were also snapped. All of that additional damage was rated EF1. Over 30 cars that were parked in the parking lot by the administrative building were damaged, and some were tossed or rolled as much as 50-100 yards - a few badly crumpled due to impacts. There were some objects driven into the ground near the parking lot as well; an emergency manager also observed a screwdriver driven into the bottom of a flipped vehicle. Some concrete curbs along the parking lot were ripped from the ground.||After moving northeast off the prison grounds, the tornado crossed some forested area near the Apalachicola River, clipping areas along the southern tip of River Road and then crossing Highway 20 just west of the Apalachicola River bridge. There were numerous healthy, large trees that were snapped in the damage path, as well as some power poles. This portion of the damage path was rated EF1 with maximum winds of around 100 MPH.  This tornado continued into Liberty county and eventually ended in Gadsden county.||Monetary damage for this tornado was estimated at around $2 million.  Structural damage to the prison was estimated at around $1.8 million, but the $1.8 million figure did not include fencing, lighting, or vehicle damage that also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.  Isolated supercells developed ahead of the main squall line, and one produced an EF2 tornado in Florida which was the longest continuous damage path in Florida since the Central Florida outbreak of February 1, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado from Calhoun and Liberty counties.  The last portion of the damage path was in southwest Gadsden County where the tornado briefly intensified and the damage path narrowed. Another corridor of more significant tree damage was noted near Sycamore Road and Winding Creek Road. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted, and several power poles were snapped. A few homes in this area had some minor damage to the exterior of the structure. Damage in this area was rated EF1. The damage path quickly narrowed thereafter, and the damage became more sporadic, with the last damaged noted along Flat Creek Road just east of Audie Clark Road.||With a total damage path length of 22.3 miles, this was the longest tornado path in the NWS Tallahassee County Warning Area (CWA) since March 1, 2007 (two tornadoes with about a 30mi path length in SE Alabama and SW Georgia during that event).||This is also the longest tornado path in the state of Florida since a February 1, 2007 tornado in Lake and Volusia Counties in central Florida (26 miles).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced penny size hail as it moved through portions of Bay county.

A cluster of slow moving thunderstorms produced some flash flooding across rural portions of Washington county.  A farmer's rain gauge measured 9 inches of rain in just a few hours, which also agreed with radar estimates.

The water was deep enough for Public Works to place barricades across the road and across other rural roads near this area.  A farmer's rain gauge measured 9 inches of rain in about 2.5 hours in this area, and radar estimated similar amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A anomolous winter weather event including sleet, freezing rain and snow flurries occurred on Jan 29th when an area of low pressure deepened offshore of the Fl/Ga Altantic coast and cold and moist air funnelled southward on the west side of the low over southeast Georgia and northeast Florida. An upper level disturbance passed over the shallow cold and moist airmass, with triggered rain mixed with a wintry mix at times during the day on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. Icing on bridges occurred, and many schools across the area closed on the 29th and on the 30th due to the potential for ice freezing on bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported sleet in Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with high winds produced damage in the Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph downed a tree on a house and car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.  Isolated supercells developed ahead of the main squall line, and one produced an EF2 tornado in Florida which was the longest continuous damage path in Florida since the Central Florida outbreak of February 1, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the Calhoun County tornado.  The tornado then moved through northwest Liberty County. We were able to determine it was a continuous damage path as there was damage noted along all of the major roadways that intersected the radar rotation track. The damage in Liberty County was limited to primarily trees, and this segment of the damage path was rated EF0 with maximum winds of 70-80 MPH. Measurements of the width of the damage path on several roads in Liberty County indicates that the tornado may have been at its widest at this point, though, with a maximum path width of around 420 yards.  This tornado then continued in Gadsden county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and blocked Winona Street at Beulah Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought record flooding along with severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and tornadoes to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major to historic flash flooding took place on the evening of April 29th through the early morning of April 30th as 12 to 22 inches of rain fell across the southern half of Santa Rosa county. The highest measured rainfall was 20.39 inches 10 miles south of Milton by a CoCoRAHS observer.||Hundreds of homes and businesses experienced significant flooding with property damage estimated to be just under $10 million. The hardest hit areas included Milton, Gulf Breeze, and Navarre. In addition, widespread infrastructure damage to roads, bridges, and drainage systems was caused by the rapid rising flood waters. Infrastructure damage is estimated to have cost around $6 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of severe storms tracked across SE Ga during the morning hours and then across NE Fl during the early to mid afternoon hours. Severe storms produced widespread wind damage with a few isolated rotating supercells. Sufficient heating near the St. Johns river basin produced elevated surface based instability during the afternoon, and updrafts were greatly enhanced by this forcing. A few isolated pre-squall line cells blossomed over Putnam...St. Johns and Flagler counties in the afternoon and produced large hail. A wall cloud was observed with a rotating storm over Putnam county, and a tornado warning was issued for this cell, however, ground based tornadic damage was not reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to dime size hail persisted for 10 minutes at the St. Johns County Emergency Operations Center near State Road 16 and Interstate 95.

The Army Corps of Engineers estimated that a permanent fix will cost up to $5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering instability from an upper level short wave trough and instability continued severe storms overnight over portions of NE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell down and crushed six cars in the Chili's parking lot on SW 36th Avenue. The cost of damage was estimated at $20,000/car for inclusion of the event in StormData.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass combined with forcing from the sea breezes to produce a pulse severe storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Summerfield. The time of damage was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer SW flow combined with a pocket of mid level dry air and cool 500 mb temps around -7 to -8 deg C interacted over an unstable airmass which spawned some scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down over a road. Time of damage based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer SW flow combined with a pocket of mid level dry air and cool 500 mb temps around -7 to -8 deg C interacted over an unstable airmass which spawned some scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down. The time of damage was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer SW flow combined with a pocket of mid level dry air and cool 500 mb temps around -7 to -8 deg C interacted over an unstable airmass which spawned some scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Taylor. The time of damage was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze thunderstorms along with ample low-level moisture produced several strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported a lightning strike to a tree. After being struck, a large tree branch fell across a residential road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze thunderstorms along with ample low-level moisture produced several strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Channing Park community Facebook site reported hail in the neighborhood. The majority of the hail was nickel size with some measuring up to quarter-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed early in the afternoon in southwest Florida. One of which produced numerous lightning strikes which resulted in the injuries and fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement and media reported a lightning strike on Fort Myers Beach. Three people were walking on the beach with thunderstorms in the area. A 41 year old male was struck and killed. Two others were injured, one critically, and were transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable southerly wind flow helped to generate numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms across southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was struck by lightning when a bolt hit a nearby tree and traveled through the ground, impacting him. He was hospitalized but released by the next day. Time is estimated based on archived lightning data from Earth Systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over Orange and Seminole Counties early in the afternoon, one of which produced a severe wind gust in Altamonte Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A WeatherFlow station near the intersections of SR-434 and SR-436 in Altamonte Springs recorded a 58 mph wind gust out of the northwest as an isolated severe thunderstorm impacted the site. Additionally, a storm report from the public estimated winds of 60 mph at the same location and time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough of low pressure across the eastern U.S. resulted in a moist and unstable environment over South Florida producing scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A United States Postal Service worker sustained serious burns as lightning struck nearby his truck. The electricity traveled through the truck as he was touching it. The location was 1781 E. Commercial Blvd. near Oakland Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough over the eastern U.S. caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN (TM) spotter reported a tornado in an un-populated area west of Clint Moore Rd. and the Florida Turnpike at 1633 EDT. The spotter reported a duration of approximately 2 minutes with no lingering funnel cloud. A member of the public and a Fish and Wildlife Service employee also reported this tornado. Since no damage was reported due to its location west of the metro area, the EF0 rating is only a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported a funnel cloud to the west of Lakewood Park in St. Lucie County and another funnel cloud was observed near Kissimmee Airport by FAA personnel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported a funnel cloud approximately five miles west southwest of Lakewood park, moving east. The funnel cloud was associated with a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment resulted in scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Broward County Sheriff Fire Rescue reports a 34-year old male was injured by lightning while sitting on a railing at a warehouse on the 100 block of Lock Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds allowed for the east coast sea breeze to push west across the Florida peninsula, developing scattered thunderstorms. One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of a downed fence in the Tyrone area of Saint Petersburg. A carport was also ripped off of a mobile home in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along sea breeze boundaries and continued through the early evening. One of these storms produced a lightning strike that injured one person in Levy County.  Another thunderstorm produced a downburst wind gust in Manatee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levy County Department of Public Safety reported a woman had been struck by lightning in Chiefland and was transported to a hospital, although injuries appeared to be minor. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along sea breeze boundaries and continued through the early evening. One of these storms produced a lightning strike that injured one person in Levy County.  Another thunderstorm produced a downburst wind gust in Manatee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport measured a 54 knot thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported a funnel cloud to the west of Lakewood Park in St. Lucie County and another funnel cloud was observed near Kissimmee Airport by FAA personnel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FAA personnel at Kissimmee Airport (KISM) observed a funnel cloud west of the airport, moving south. The funnel cloud was associated with a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere coupled with a series of upper level disturbances resulted in several persistent rainbands. Significant urban and small stream flooding affected portions of Melbourne/Palm Bay and Lake Mary/Winter Park. Flash flooding resulted in rapid water accumulation in New Smyrna Beach and from Holly Hill to Daytona Beach to Port Orange, where numerous roadways became impassible and over 100 homes sustained damage from the rising flood waters. Rainfall totals during the 24-hour period of greatest impact reached 6-12 inches. Flood damage estimates within Volusia County was 3.65 million dollars. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain totals of 4 to 7 inches accumulated in less than two hours due to persistent thunderstorms. Flooding of many roadways caused them to become temporarily impassable. Two county fire stations, and several homes and business became isolated due to high levels of standing water and some minor water damage was reported. The most impacted areas included Lake Mary, Casselberry, Ovideo, Winter Springs and Longwood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain totals of 3 to 6 inches accumulated in less than two hours due to persistent thunderstorms. Flooding of several roadways caused them to become temporarily impassable. Several homes became isolated due to high levels of standing water. The most impacted areas included Winter Park, Azalea Park and Maitland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough and cold brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall to the area.  There were several tornadoes confirmed across northwest Florida.  All of the tornadoes were rated EF0, and most of them occurred in forested areas with no structures impacted.  However, there was some minor damage to structures around Cottondale in Jackson county and Sumatra in Liberty county.  All of the tornadoes exhibited tornadic debris signatures using dual-pol radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Mixon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western Florida peninsula producing scattered thunderstorms over southwest Florida early in the afternoon. One of these storms caused a damaging wind gust in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was received via social media of an uprooted tree on Three Oaks Parkway, about a quarter mile north of Estero Parkway.  Time was estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike killed a 71 year old man while he was out in a field picking blueberries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike killed a 71 year old man while he was out in a field picking blueberries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A local man was pulled from the surf and died after being caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51 year old male from Tennessee died of drowning after being caught in a rip current at an unguarded beach. Ocean rescue personnel observed dangerous rip currents at the time of the drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary produced localized areas of heavy rain which caused minor flooding in low-lying areas as well as along some area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer south of Clearwater measured 5.9 inches of rain during the 4 day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, early-season cold front pushed through the area during the morning hours on 10/4. Winds quickly became onshore at 15 to 20 mph increasing the threat for rip currents along area beaches. Several water rescues had taken place in the surrounding area. A Rip Current Advisory was in effect at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 55-year-old man drowned off the coast of Treasure Island, most likely due to strong rip currents. Authorities were called to the 8400 block of Gulf Boulevard around 3 PM after receiving a 911 call. The call came shortly after beach-walkers spotted an unconscious body floating in the water near the beach and carried it to shore. First responders performed CPR on the man but they could not save him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on a seasonably hot, muggy day with a few storms becoming severe.  An unusual lightning event occurred in Jackson county where lightning struck a moving vehicle and actually injured the occupant inside.  The vehicle's electrical system was reported to have significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was struck by lightning on I-10 near the Grand Ridge exit.  Significant damage occurred to the vehicle and the person inside was transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed just west of the intracoastal waters in Fort Pierce following a boundary collision with the east coast sea breeze. A brief landspout/tornado developed near US-1 and Indrio Road along the outflow boundary of one of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist near the intersection of Highway US-1 and Indrio Road in Fort Pierce observed a brief touchdown of a weak landspout/tornado that crossed US-1. No damage was reported and an examination of video relayed via social media suggests maximum winds were below 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere coupled with a series of upper level disturbances resulted in several persistent rainbands. Significant urban and small stream flooding affected portions of Melbourne/Palm Bay and Lake Mary/Winter Park. Flash flooding resulted in rapid water accumulation in New Smyrna Beach and from Holly Hill to Daytona Beach to Port Orange, where numerous roadways became impassible and over 100 homes sustained damage from the rising flood waters. Rainfall totals during the 24-hour period of greatest impact reached 6-12 inches. Flood damage estimates within Volusia County was 3.65 million dollars. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small area of persistent rainbands trained across New Smryna Beach during the late afternoon and early early evening. Rainfall totals reached 8 to 12 inches, resulting in significant urban and roadway flooding. Retention ponds and drainage canals overflowed and rising water entered 7 single family homes and one public building. Four of these structures experienced major damage, 3 had minor damage and one was affected. Estimated damage was over 500 thousand dollars. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon thunderstorms produced lightning over southern sections of Pinellas County. A man on a motorcycle was struck by lightning just northeast of Gulfport. Emergency responders were sent to the scene and the man refused to be taken to the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The state watch office reported a man on a motorcycle was struck by lightning near Gulfport. The man was treated on-scene for his injuries and refused to be taken to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere coupled with a series of upper level disturbances resulted in several persistent rainbands. Significant urban and small stream flooding affected portions of Melbourne/Palm Bay and Lake Mary/Winter Park. Flash flooding resulted in rapid water accumulation in New Smyrna Beach and from Holly Hill to Daytona Beach to Port Orange, where numerous roadways became impassible and over 100 homes sustained damage from the rising flood waters. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure located over Central Florida resulted in abundant tropical moisture flowing into the southern peninsula with very heavy rain bands setting up along the southwest coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense rainfall associated with several bands of thunderstorms developed across much of the Naples area during the early afternoon. The band moved little between 1230 EDT and 1500 EDT with the training of cells leading to copious rain amounts and severe street flooding in parts of Naples and Golden Gate. The first report of flooding was received from a trained spotter at 1405 EDT and stated that at least two feet of water was on roads near Airport Road and Mercantile Avenue with cars stalled out. The Collier County Sheriffs Office reported at around 1510 EDT that there was severe flooding and stalled vehicles from Collier Blvd. to Tamiami Trail with some roads closed. Rainfall totals included 7 inches at Naples Beach Hotel and Club with 6.73 inches at the Naples Municipal Airport. A trained spotter measured 4.21 inches in just under an hour in the area of Airport and Pine Ridge Roads.||A few businesses had water enter their structures, with one business estimating $12,000 in damage. Over 300 cars were towed from area streets due to stalling in deep water. Damage total indicated in this report is estimated and based on number of cars stalled as well as the damage to businesses from water intrusion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed on the east coast sea breeze and produced very localized wind damage in Casselberry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A manufactured home on Laura Street in the Lake Kathryn Estates subdivision had a large portion of its metal roof sheered off due to strong winds. Several carports attached to homes nearby experienced minor damage. Damage photos suggested peak winds less than 58 mph. The time of damage was estimated by radar.

There were several tornadoes confirmed across northwest Florida.  All of the tornadoes were rated EF0, and most of them occurred in forested areas with no structures impacted.  However, there was some minor damage to structures around Cottondale in Jackson county and Sumatra in Liberty county.  All of the tornadoes exhibited tornadic debris signatures using dual-pol radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were stalled in high water on roads near the Tallahassee Mall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported two trees down south of Chiefland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer high pressure was NW of the area with mean layer flow form the ENE. Sea breeze thunderstorms along the Atlantic coast developed during the early afternoon, and produced some lightning damage in Duval county, including a wildfire near Pumpkin Hill Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to a power sub-station at the corner of Old St. Augustine Road near Gran Bay Parkway caused widespread power outages and a transformer fire. About 6000 homes were without power, and traffic lights along several main Southsite Roads were inoperable. The cost of damage was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer high pressure was NW of the area with mean layer flow form the ENE. Sea breeze thunderstorms along the Atlantic coast developed during the early afternoon, and produced some lightning damage in Duval county, including a wildfire near Pumpkin Hill Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on U.S. Highway 301 near Meadows Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly-stationary frontal boundary across South Florida led to periods of torrential downpours across central and northern Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall during the afternoon hours led to flash flooding in and near downtown West Palm Beach and as far north as Riviera Beach. Palm Beach County Sheriff's Office closed several roads in the area, including the area of Australian Avenue and 45th Street in West Palm Beach. Main impact was from impassable streets with no reports of significant structural flooding. Measured rainfall amounts were in the 5 to 8 inch range in less than 6 hours, with rainfall rates of 4-5 inches per hour during the height of the event. Maximum measured rainfall total was 8.78 inches in the northern West Palm Beach area near the Intracoastal Waterway.||Damage total is estimated and based primarily on reports of impassable streets and stalled vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer high pressure was NW of the area with mean layer flow form the ENE. Sea breeze thunderstorms along the Atlantic coast developed during the early afternoon, and produced some lightning damage in Duval county, including a wildfire near Pumpkin Hill Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sea breeze thunderstorm produced a lightning strike which caused a brush fire in the Pumpkin Hill Creek Preserve. The fire grew to approximately 3.6 acres near Cedar Point Road. There was no threat to life.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted surface trough drifted westward over the area and combined with high moisture content and diurnal instability, locally heavy rainfall resulted from afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported near the intersection of State Road 100 and Commercial Road with about 3-4 inches of standing water on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted surface trough drifted westward over the area and combined with high moisture content and diurnal instability, locally heavy rainfall resulted from afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Secondary roads had standing water on them in the Maddox area. A few locations were not passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad low pressure system slowly meandered over the forecast area Sept. 6th through the 8th. On the night of the 6th and 7th a widespread area of nocturnal rain broke out across inland NE Florida, which dumped rainfall amounts of 8-14 inches over portions of Columbia County, especially from Lake City southward toward Columbia. Several homes were flooded with numerous road closures. Flash flood warnings were issued for almost the same area each morning for this area of Columbia county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding caused numerous road closures and flooded approximately 5-6 homes. One flooded home was located at SW Fedora Way, and residents used canoes and kayaks to travel streets. There was about 1 foot of standing water inside the home. Portions of Interstate 10 near mile marker 296 were closed due to flooded roads. Columbia City Public Works installed pumps at the intersection of Nightshade and Broadleaf Drives due to flooded roads and water nearing homes. Portions of State Road 47 just south of Watson Road  were covered in several inches of standing waters. |Radar estimated about 8-13 inches between Lake City and Columbia, just south of Lake City. The cost of property damage is unknown at this time. The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion of this event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough and cold brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall to the area.  There were several tornadoes confirmed across northwest Florida.  All of the tornadoes were rated EF0, and most of them occurred in forested areas with no structures impacted.  However, there was some minor damage to structures around Cottondale in Jackson county and Sumatra in Liberty county.  All of the tornadoes exhibited tornadic debris signatures using dual-pol radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KTLH dual-pol radar detected a tornadic debris signature at this location.  This is in the middle of the Apalachicola National Forest with no damage to structures reported.  The tornado was rated EF0 based on tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough and cold brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall to the area.  There were several tornadoes confirmed across northwest Florida.  All of the tornadoes were rated EF0, and most of them occurred in forested areas with no structures impacted.  However, there was some minor damage to structures around Cottondale in Jackson county and Sumatra in Liberty county.  All of the tornadoes exhibited tornadic debris signatures using dual-pol radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the damage from this tornado was limited to trees.  However, one house had a garage door blown in and some of the roof structure removed.  The tornado was rated EF0 with winds estimated at 75 to 80 mph.  A small debris signature was also seen on KEVX dual-pol radar.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and a roof was blown off a shed in the vicinity of Sumatra.  A tornadic debris signature was also seen by KTLH radar.  This tornado was rated EF0 with winds of 65 to 75 mph.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep column moisture and slow storm motion setup an environment for long durations of heavy rainfall and minor flooding. Radar precipitation estimates reached as high as five inches in many locations around west-central Florida. Standing water was reported to have entered a building in Polk County, and several cars became stranded in deep water covering roadways in Hillsborough County.  Additionally, the rain caused the roof of an apartment building in Manatee County to collapse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed reports and pictures of cars stranded in water covering the roadway at the intersection of Hesperides and Lambright Streets in Tampa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer troughing was over the forecast area, as well as mid level dry air. A short wave trough traversed NE Florida in the afternoon, and this features combined with the forcing from the sea breezes and diurnal instability produced a few severe storms that produced gusty winds over NE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Fort McCoy. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow brought waves of heavy rainfall that became nearly stationary over Palm Coast during the early afternoon on Sept. 26th. Heavy rainfall continued to impact NE Flagler County from Palm Coast to Bunnell, with storm total amounts of 7-10 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training, slow moving showers and thunderstorms within a onshore flow caused flash flooding in the Palm Coast area of Flagler County. Rainfall started in the early afternoon, when a spotter in Palm Coast reported 3.56 of rainfall within 3 hours between 12-3 pm. By 3:55 pm, several vehicles and a school bus were stranded in water over the roadway in the western section of Palm Coast. By 4:15 pm, 4.41 inches of rainfall was measured within 3 hours on Bannerword Lane in Palm Coast. Later that evening around 9 pm, county officials reported the road was washed out at the intersection of Hickory and Walnut Streets to the intersection of Walnut and Fig Streets in Bunnell. Several roads in the W-Section of Palm Coast including Woodward Lane, Woodside Drive, Woodshire Land and the north end of Woodhaven Drive were closed due to flooded roads. At 10:35 pm, observation stations in west Palm Coast measured 5.83-5.88 of rainfall within 12 hours. At 10:48 pm, the county EM reported about 2 feet of standing water along portions of Florida Pack Drive in Palm Coast. About 6 structures had minor damage from flood waters. The cost of damage was unknown, and it was estimated for this event to be included in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 3.56 within a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough and cold brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall to the area.  There were several tornadoes confirmed across northwest Florida.  All of the tornadoes were rated EF0, and most of them occurred in forested areas with no structures impacted.  However, there was some minor damage to structures around Cottondale in Jackson county and Sumatra in Liberty county.  All of the tornadoes exhibited tornadic debris signatures using dual-pol radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KTLH and KEVX dual-pol radars detected a tornadic debris signature at this location.  In addition, a member of the media conducted a survey of the area using an aerial drone. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area moving east off the east-central Florida coast in combination with a long fetch of moderate to strong northeast winds from off the New England coast all the way to South Florida led to an extended period of high surf along the SE Florida coast, especially in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large northerly swells pounded the Palm Beach County coast for over 24 hours due to a long fetch of northeast winds from off the New England coast to South Florida. Surf heights were at least 8 feet high and likely above 10 feet in some areas. This high surf led to significant beach erosion, as well as damage to both Juno Beach and Lake Worth piers where wooden panels popped out of the pier due to the large and powerful waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 70 mph downed trees and damaged buildings in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 70 mph downed trees and power lines. The high winds also damaged 3 houses and 2 barns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms associated with a stalled boundary across the region produced a few thunderstorms across the Bay Area. One of which produced a lightning strike near Raymond James Stadium shortly after the conclusion of the Tampa Bay Buccaneers football game.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida State Watch Office relayed a report from Tampa Fire Rescue of a lightning strike across the street from Raymond James Stadium shortly after the conclusion of the Buccaneers football game. Lightning detection networks show that this was one of the first lightning strikes in the area. Seven people were transported to a nearby hospital with another four taking themselves to the hospital for treatment. One person was said to be in critical condition while being transported to a nearby hospital. Minor damage occurred to the car the victims were around as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the late evening. As these storms moved across Brevard County, they intensified and became severe, producing a strong wind gusts and isolated damage in Central Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated near 60 mph produced damage to portions of the Merritt Island High School sport complex. The baseball field dugout was blown over and a portion of the football locker room awning was detached from the roof. A tennis court fence and baseball field center fence were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first taste of fall arrived in the area behind a cold front, which moved through the region on October 3rd.  There was sufficient instability and shear for a line of storms to move across portions of the Florida panhandle.  Most of the severe storms stayed in southern Georgia, but one storm did blow some trees down in Walton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Old Jolly Bay Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pre-frontal squall line crossed the area late morning through mid afternoon. Strong wind shear and helicity promoted rotation cells as well as bowing line segments. One comma-head storm formed along the Interstate 10 corridor near Live Oak, and this cell raced eastward toward Jacksonville. The storm had a long-lived mesocylone, and the rear flank downdraft descended downward several times causing brief periods of strong low level rotation which caused a few tornado touchdowns. The RFD produced strong winds of 50-80 mph and caused widespread tree damage and some structural damage as the comma-head raced across north Florida, including the Jacksonville metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen near Sandalwood High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the area as well as a developing area of low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across east central Florida.  Several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms led to minor flooding across parts of the area as well as some wind damage to a manufactured home community in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm with estimated winds of 60 mph produced minor wind damage to a few mobile homes in the Lakeshore Landings community in Orlando. A porch was torn off one of the homes and flipped onto its roof. Several other homes sustained minor damage to soffits and siding, and several trees were damaged.

Flooding of many roadways caused them to become temporarily impassable.

The RFD produced strong winds of 50-80 mph and caused widespread tree damage and some structural damage as the comma-head raced across north Florida, including the Jacksonville metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down throughout Lakeside. One oak tree was down on a home along Cedar Crest Drive. Power lines were blown down onto railroad tracks at Doctors Lake and Moody Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the area as well as a developing area of low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across east central Florida.  Several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms led to minor flooding across parts of the area as well as some wind damage to a manufactured home community in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rainfall occurred across much of Volusia County during the day on the 25th, continuing into the 26th. Two day rainfall totals were 4 to 8 inches, with the highest totals across coastal areas from Edgewater to Daytona Beach. These rainfall totals resulted in minor road flooding and closures along several streets in Edgewater, New Smyrna Beach and Daytona Beach. Minor flooding of three homes occurred in New Smyrna Beach, with estimated damage around 15 thousand dollars. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere coupled with a series of upper level disturbances resulted in several persistent rainbands. Significant urban and small stream flooding affected portions of Melbourne/Palm Bay and Lake Mary/Winter Park. Flash flooding resulted in rapid water accumulation in New Smyrna Beach and from Holly Hill to Daytona Beach to Port Orange, where numerous roadways became impassible and over 100 homes sustained damage from the rising flood waters. Rainfall totals during the 24-hour period of greatest impact reached 6-12 inches. Flood damage estimates within Volusia County was 3.65 million dollars. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of thunderstorms produced 3 to 6 inches of rain in less than two hours across West Melbourne and Palm Bay. Many roadways become temporarily impassable and a van became stuck in a rain-filled median on Florida Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal squall line intensified as it moved into east central Florida. An apartment building in Celebration was destroyed by a lightning-induced fire, and severe  thunderstorm wind gusts occurred in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A new, 24-unit apartment building within the Evander Square Apartment Complex in the town of Celebration was destroyed by a fire after being struck by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal squall line intensified as it moved into east central Florida. An apartment building in Celebration was destroyed by a lightning-induced fire, and severe  thunderstorm wind gusts occurred in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USAF Tower 1007 near Port St. Johns recorded a 64 mph wind gust from the WNW as a thunderstorm moved through the area. In addition, USAF Towers 211 and 311, nearby at Playalinda Beach, reported 64 mph and 59 mph wind gusts at 1440 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure and its associated strong cold front moved across South Florida causing an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop over northeast Miami-Dade County and move into southern Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Miami-Dade Fire Rescue at Opa-Locka Executive Airport reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 61 knots, or 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing short wave trough combined with a lingering surface trough and sea breezes produced strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east and west coast sea-breezes collided over the interior of east central Florida and produced a large north to south oriented band of thunderstorms from Volusia County, south to Okeechobee County. Two manufactured homes in Zellwood were damaged from an isolated severe thunderstorm embedded within the line, with produced a microbust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated winds of 65-70 mph produced damage to two manufactured homes in the Zellwood Station subdivision in Zellwood. One home had a large portion of its metal roof peeled off and its carport destroyed. The other home sustained minor damage to both its roof and carport. Nearby, a small shed was damaged and a few tree branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing short wave trough combined with a lingering surface trough and sea breezes produced strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported on Interstate 10 near Stockton Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers moved northeast across Volusia and Brevard Counties. A severe thunderstorm developed near the northern edge of the line and caused localized wind damage near the Daytona Beach International Airport and Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University Campus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst produced estimated winds of 70 mph near the Daytona Beach International Airport. The downburst flipped a Cessna-172 plane taxiing on the runway of Daytona Beach International Airport. The two occupants on board were uninjured. Several windows were shattered in a nearby office building and large tree branches were downed at the Embry Riddle Aeronautical University. Downed power lines and trees occurred within residential areas just east of the campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in the Northern Gulf of Mexico and an associated cold front allowed for the development of a squall line during the late morning and early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hernando county emergency manager forwarded pictures of damage. Most damage appears to be sub-severe with damage to patio screens and mobile homes. However, there were some indications that winds may briefly have been in the 55 to 65 mph range as seen in pictures of downed, healthy trees and some minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds caused a large tree to fall onto a home in Clermont, resulting in minor damage. Later in the day, thunderstorms moving into Brevard County intensified after interacting with the east coast sea breeze and produced severe wind gusts near Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USAF Tower 421 measured a 72 mph wind gust from the west as a severe thunderstorm moved from land to sea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds caused a large tree to fall onto a home in Clermont, resulting in minor damage. Later in the day, thunderstorms moving into Brevard County intensified after interacting with the east coast sea breeze and produced severe wind gusts near Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds associated with thunderstorms a few miles to the west and south of Ferndale caused a giant oak tree to topple onto a home. The tree fell on the garage, and the rest of the home was not damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed in a warm and unstable environment ahead of a cold front over the eastern Gulf of Mexico. A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in the city of Sanford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Orlando TV station relayed a report from the public of quarter-sized hail at the Sanford Memorial Stadium.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong long wave trough was centered over the mid Mississippi Valley extending south to the Gulf coast with several shortwaves rotating around it.  During the April 29-30 time period, a strong MCS developed along the Gulf coast and trained across the area, producing extremely heavy rainfall rates and amounts on top of what had already been a very wet month with additional rainfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches common across the area.  This led to serious flooding across portions of the Florida panhandle with several million dollars in damage reported, mainly to roads.  However, several structures were also impacted by flood waters, especially in Walton county, and a FEMA disaster declaration was obtained across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down a half mile or so east of Highway 77 south of Graceville.  Damage was mostly to trees, but several homes were also damaged.  One was severely damaged when a large oak tree toppled onto it.  Damage to trees and to one home east of Highway 77 was consistent with EF1 damage with peak winds around 110 mph.  This tornado also exhibited a debris signature on dual-pol radar.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local TV station reported a large tree snapped at its base in Winter Haven. The tree landed on a Jeep causing minor damage to the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong long wave trough was centered over the mid Mississippi Valley extending south to the Gulf coast with several shortwaves rotating around it. This led to serious flooding across portions of the Florida panhandle with several million dollars in damage reported, mainly to roads.  However, several structures were also impacted by flood waters, especially in Walton county, and a FEMA disaster declaration was obtained across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado was confirmed in Jackson county starting along Highway 231 southeast of Campbellton with a few trees blown over and snapped.  There was evidence of sporadic damage to trees to the north-northeast.  The second, and more significant area of damage of damage was to a grove of pine trees east of Campbellton along Highway 2.  There was clear evidence of a tornado at this location consistent with higher end EF1 damage.  There was no evidence of damage very far to the northeast of this road.  Peak winds were estimated to be around 110 mph, and this tornado produced a debris signature on dual-pol radar.  Monetary damage was estimated.

During the April 29-30 time period, a strong MCS developed along the Gulf coast and trained across the area, producing extremely heavy rainfall rates and amounts on top of what had already been a very wet month with additional rainfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches common across the area.  This led to serious flooding across portions of the Florida panhandle with several million dollars in damage reported, mainly to roads.  However, several structures were also impacted by flood waters, especially in Walton county, and a FEMA disaster declaration was obtained across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five trees were blown down on Dead River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong long wave trough was centered over the mid Mississippi Valley extending south to the Gulf coast with several shortwaves rotating around it.  During the April 29-30 time period, a strong MCS developed along the Gulf coast and trained across the area, producing extremely heavy rainfall rates and amounts on top of what had already been a very wet month with additional rainfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches common across the area.  This led to serious flooding across portions of the Florida panhandle with several million dollars in damage reported, mainly to roads.  However, several structures were also impacted by flood waters, especially in Walton county, and a FEMA disaster declaration was obtained across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred across Jackson county at the end of the month due to a combination of very heavy rainfall and already saturated conditions. Many roads in the county sustained damage with damage estimated at $6,691,424.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved slowly northwest over southeast Okeechobee County and produced a brief, weak EF-0 tornado. The tornado damaged several trees and destroyed a small storage shed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low-end EF-0 tornado touched down in rural Okeechobee, just south of SE 8th Street, crossed the street, then moved through a line of trees into an open field. Several trees were toppled and a 20 foot by 20 foot light weight shed in the field experienced a direct hit from the tornado and was destroyed. Some light metal debris was lofted into nearby pine trees. After an examination of damage photos, maximum winds were estimated at 60-65 mph. Prior to the damage, several citizens reported seeing a funnel cloud or tornado in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several motorists witnessed the touchdown of a very weak and short-lived landspout/tornado along State Road 520, just north of the State Road 528 intersection within eastern Orange County. No damage occurred.

No damage was reported due to the landspout/tornado even though it crossed a line of trees either side of the highway. An examination of video and photographs and the lack of damage suggest maximum winds below 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down within a rural airpark and affected adjacent neighborhoods. Several buildings and small planes were damaged at the airpark, and many trees were damaged or uprooted within the adjacent neighborhood as the tornado tracked northeast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down within a rural airpark and affected adjacent neighborhoods. Several buildings and small planes were damaged at the airpark, and many trees were damaged or uprooted within the adjacent neighborhood as the tornado tracked northeast. The thunderstorm which produced the tornado was also responsible for wind damage to a fence and the roof of an apartment building on the barrier island in New Smyrna Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down and reached maximum intensity (estimated 100-105 mph) within the Massey Ranch Airpark in Edgewater where three small aircraft were flipped and damaged and three large hangars sustained major structural damage. Several people were inside the hangars at the time, but were uninjured. The tornado continued northeast and uprooted or damaged many trees, caused minor damage to the roofs of several homes and destroyed sheds within the adjacent Wildwood neighborhood. Roof damage to several of the homes was due to falling trees. Several cars were damaged after being impacted by debris. Farther downstream, sporadic, less significant tree damage occurred. The tornado lifted near the Halifax River, approximately 1.7 miles from where it touched down. Property damage was estimated at $723,000, excluding damage to the aircraft. DI21 DOD3 EXP-UB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold, surface high pressure settled over south Florida in the wake of a strong cold front that moved across the area on January 17th. Calm winds and clear skies led to a very cold night and early morning across interior south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing over interior sections of Palm Beach County near Lake Okeechobee on the early morning of January 19th. Temperatures just outside Belle Glade at the FAWN station dropped to 30 degrees, with unofficial readings in the mid to upper 20s from South Bay to Pahokee. This led to some damage to the vegetable crop in western Palm Beach County, such as corn and lettuce. An estimated 700 acres of corn were lost near South Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across South Florida and interacted with a very moist and tropical airmass to produce historic rainfall amounts across portions of eastern Palm Beach County during the late evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms began to develop over the east coast metropolitan area of Palm Beach County during the evening hours and began to train across the same areas for the next several hours. As a result, high rainfall amounts were observed from Delray Beach to Jupiter. The heaviest rain fell from about 8 PM to Midnight. The area from Kings Point to Boynton Beach, Hypoluxo and Town of Palm Beach received the heaviest amounts of 10 to 20 inches, with the maximum amount reported from a mesonet site in Boynton Beach just east of I-95 and north of Gateway Boulevard receiving an astonishing 22.21 inches in about a 6-hour period. Other reports were 16.03 inches just southwest of Palm Beach, 15.04 inches at Lantana and 13.13 inches at Boynton Beach. These heavy rainfall amounts also resulted in several road closures including all lanes of I-95 in Boynton Beach between mile markers 56 and 60  with many cars on side streets reported being flooded. Tragically, two fatalities were also reported the following morning: a 56-year old female drove her vehicle off a flooded roadway in Delray Beach and into a lake and also in Delray Beach, a 90-year old male drowned after slipping into a canal while walking down a street. Several businesses and residences also reported inundation during the event but little damage was reported, including along Worth Avenue in Palm Beach, Boynton Beach east of I-95 and in parts of Delray Beach. ||Approximately 200 homes had water intrusion, although the vast majority of these only had minor flooding. Damage totals indicated are estimated based on number of homes with water intrusion and level of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line, preceding a cold front, crossed east-central Florida during the afternoon. A bow echo along the leading edge of the squall line moved rapidly east across southern Orange County. A swath of winds 45-60 mph accompanied the bow echo and produced tree damage along its path. A book-end vortex rapidly evolved at the northern end of the echo, and resulted in an EF-0 tornado within the corridor of straight-line winds. The squall line also produced minor wind damage in Brevard County. Large hail fell in the Sanford area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the squall line crossed southern Brevard County, damaging winds affected portions of Melbourne and Palm Bay and produced minor wind damage. Broadcast media relayed a photo of damage to the metal roof of a mobile home in the Lakewood Village subdivision in Melbourne. Two blocks away, a small portion of a roof on a single family home was peeled back. Power lines were downed from severe thunderstorm winds on Palm Bay Road west of Interstate 95.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bow echo associated with a severe thunderstorm travelled rapidly east across southern Orange County. A NWS Storm Survey revealed an 8-mile long swath of straight-line (west-to-east) damage to trees, fences and signs. The width of the corridor of winds estimated between 45 and 60 mph varied between one-half mile and one-mile. Several homes and vehicles were damaged due to falling trees and tree branches.
